ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for braised eggplant with minced meat; mapped to the nearest parent class 'braised food product' (FOODON_03311737) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Aubergine (eggplant), cooked — used as the primary vegetable component anchor for this composite braised dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for braised eggplant with minced meat; A0CKP (cooked aubergine) is used as the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor. The minced meat component is not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of braised eggplant with minced meat is approximately 350 g, typically consumed as a main dish alongside steamed white rice in a Chinese-Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Braised Eggplant with Minced Meat (estimated from component analysis); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228) and cooked ground pork (FDC ID 167903)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for braised eggplant with minced meat as a composite dish was found. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central data for cooked eggplant and cooked ground pork. The input values are plausible for a braised eggplant and minced pork dish at the stated macronutrient ratios. Values are retained with medium confidence and documented as estimated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on cooked eggplant (GI ~15) and mixed meat-vegetable braised dish profile; composite GI estimated at 45 reflecting added sauces and starch thickener.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised eggplant with minced meat as a composite dish. Eggplant has a published GI of approximately 15 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite dish GI is estimated at 45 (low category) accounting for the addition of oyster sauce (contains sugar), cornstarch thickener, and the overall mixed meat-vegetable dish profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 11.54 g/100g = 40.4 g) / 100 = 18.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 276.9 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 969 mg sodium, representing 42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce when ordering, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickled vegetables or salted fish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (11.54 g/100g) with a low estimated GI of 45; however, a 350 g serving delivers approximately 40 g of carbohydrate, and the dish is typically consumed with white rice, substantially increasing the total glycaemic load of the meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish without rice or with a reduced rice portion (≤100 g cooked), request reduced oyster sauce to limit added sugars, and monitor total carbohydrate intake for the meal to remain within their individualised carbohydrate target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.31 g/100g and sodium at 276.9 mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; a 350 g serving provides approximately 8.1 g saturated fat (40% of the 20 g daily limit) and 969 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request leaner minced meat (chicken or turkey) as a substitute for pork, ask for reduced oil in preparation, limit portion to 200–250 g, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-saturated-fat foods in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in solanine-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; minced pork also contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid this dish; others should request substitution of minced pork with chicken or tofu and ask for chilli to be omitted to reduce nightshade and pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Eggplant with Minced Meat (红烧茄子肉末, Hóng shāo qiézi ròumò) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean home-style and hawker dish featuring tender eggplant (brinjal/aubergine) slow-braised in a rich, savoury sauce together with seasoned minced pork. The eggplant is typically cut into batons or wedges, lightly pan-fried or wok-seared until golden, then braised with minced pork in a sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, and sometimes chilli, thickened with cornstarch to produce a glossy, umami-rich coating. The result is a dish with a silky, melt-in-the-mouth eggplant texture, a savoury-sweet sauce, and a mild heat from chilli. It is almost always served over steamed white rice and is a staple of Chinese family meals and economic rice (cai fan) stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 131 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 6.9 g fat (2.3 g saturated), and 11.5 g carbohydrate with 1.2 g dietary fibre and 277 mg sodium. The sodium content is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low category), but the glycaemic load of a full plate serving (350 g) is approximately 18 g, rising substantially when consumed with rice. The dish is not suitable for strict halal diets unless pork is substituted, and contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce and oyster sauce. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Chinese-Singaporean cuisine, commonly found at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and home kitchens throughout Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an ai_generated input record with confidence 0.7 and provenance from a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central data for cooked eggplant and cooked ground pork and are plausible for this dish type. No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for this composite dish was found. Confidence is therefore set to medium reflecting estimated rather than directly sourced nutrient data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from ai_generated input (GrabFood provenance, confidence 0.7); GrabFood is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (cooked eggplant FDC ID 169228; cooked ground pork FDC ID 167903) and are retained as plausible estimates. Source match type is 'estimated'. A direct HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for this exact composite dish should be sought in future review cycles to upgrade confidence to 'high'. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite braised dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as a single composite entry as of April 2026. 3. Glycemic index is an analogous estimate (GI 45, low category) based on published GI of cooked eggplant (~15, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted upward for the composite dish profile including oyster sauce (added sugar) and cornstarch thickener. No direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. Two portions are noted in the input: 1 plate (350 g, used as default serving for nutrient and GL calculations) and 1 set meal (650 g including noodles/rice and drink). The set meal GL would be substantially higher than the 18.2 g reported for the plate-only serving. 5. Halal status is marked false because the standard preparation uses minced pork; halal variants using minced chicken or beef exist and would be suitable. 6. The input health_suitability section referenced a 'noodle component' and 'beverage' as part of the dish — these appear to relate to the set meal portion (650 g) rather than the dish itself (350 g). Health context in this record is assessed for the dish alone (350 g plate serving). 7. Glycemic load per serving (350 g plate): (GI 45 × 40.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 18.2 g.
